speaker: interviewer
text: --an interview with Manya Winshell. Manya, before we continue into your journey to the United States, I'm going to take you back a little bit. And you had an experience in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Uzbekistan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always with-- with my baby. My husband was out working in the fields there, the kolkhoz. So I was sitting with him. And all of a sudden, I see something crawling close to the wall. And that was a big, heavy snake. I don't know what kind of snake it was. But it was really scary. And I couldn't move. I couldn't tell the baby anything. And somehow, this snake went and went out. At the same time, we had an earthquake there. And I was alone with the baby, too. So I-- it start-- everything started to fly from one side to the other. And we were-- and I managed to get outside. It didn't take long.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an earthquake was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a big one, but an earthquake. And when I went out, the Uzbeks, the people, were all lying on the ground. They knew what to do. They were lying on the ground because that place-- that village-- was once had an earthquake. And they all perished there. So they were scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are talking now, we're traveling back now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the United States. It has taken you many days.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, March 23rd. But when I arrived here, I had my family waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city did you arrive in?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York. And I had my mother's brother, my mother's sister. We didn't need any help from for anybody, from [? joint ?] or anything, because they had for us ready my aunt's house a room with furniture, with everything. So we stayed there more than a month with my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of New York was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bronx.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed-- you moved in with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your mother's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the-- my husband went looking for work. He found some work for $27 a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he worked for Carvel at that time. Whatever he could get.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think he-- he worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carvel.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ice cream company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, in the company. In the factory, I think, where they made the ice cream. And the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was $27 A week. But we managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live with your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: One month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my-- my uncle's-- my aunt-- my uncle's wife-- found us a place on 200 Street near Marshall Park across from the botanical garden. And that's where we lived for a while. On the fifth floor. Had to walk five floors. We stayed there a year and a half. And after that, we moved by the Grand Concourse, [? Weeks ?] Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you moved your first time into your own apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were happy that we have our apartment, but it was very hard. On the fifth floor. And the no washing machine. I had to go to five floors to bring to the laundromat my clothes-- it wasn't easy. It was very hard. But thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, your son was going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight years old. He went to school. He went to school. And he did very well, because he graduated public school, junior high school. Went to high school of science, which you had to be smart to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you adjust to life after what you had been through? How did you adjust?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very easy. From bad times to better times, it's easier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing in the United States was that-- the language. I had to learn English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you first came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in March. In June, I went to school, evening school. My husband stayed with the children and I went to school. It took me a year and a half-- two years, let's say. And I got my elementary school diploma. And then my husband went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for your husband working at that time? What did he do once you moved into your apartment in the Bronx? What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- all kinds of jobs, he had. Then he had in a metal factory, which he was getting already $50 a-- a week, which was already good. And somehow, we saved up some money with-- with the low pay. Saved up $5,000 and bought a candy store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he open the candy store?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Bronx. Are you familiar with the Bronx? The Bronx, right behind the courthouse, Gerard Avenue. And it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your life change once you were the proprietor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it changed. We made some money. I gave the children education. My children had everything they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from that first apartment, you opened up your little candy shop. And did you move again after that, and to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Gerard. Very nice apartment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Somehow, we worked ourselves up. Then we had another store. We sold the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we built a store in Manhattan. And 28th Street and 3rd Avenue, a greeting card shop. Hallmark cards and Bartons chocolate. Which we were doing OK. What about your living conditions? What happened then after you moved your store to Manhattan?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Gerard Avenue. We had a very nice apartment there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have the candy store-- the card store?

speaker: interviewee
text: The card store until 1974. In 1974, we sold it. My daughter was married already. And we moved to Florida. That's where we were. My whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the experience of living through what you lived through change you as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It changed me in some ways because I know that we have to appreciate what we have. After going through all the things I went through, I was happy with everything I had. Because I used to dream if I still-- if I'll have a bed one day. You know, we were homeless. There's no beds. So I appreciated everything after that. And the most important thing to me was to bring up my children the right way, which I did, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tell your children about your experiences? What did you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them everything. I told them. They should know that nobody is sure with anything. I was happy with my child, with my husband. And all of a sudden we have to leave everything and run. So-- so I told them that. They should know. That nobody is sure with anything. And the most important thing is education.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children's education is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what you put in in there, nobody can take away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the most important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your feelings about religion and God today?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, I was never very religious. I believed in God. There's a God. But after the war, after the six million Jews were killed, I question everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why have you come forward to talk about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think it's later than you think. If I don't tell my story now, when I'm going to tell it? And I want my children, my grandchildren, my great grandchildren, to know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son married at what age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And does he have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He's got two-- two childrens. Got the-- a son and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jason and Jamie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me at what-- when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jason was born in 1962. And Jamie was born in 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter, you gave a good education as well. Can you talk about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. As much as I could. As much they wanted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We tried our best. My husband was a wonderful person. We went through hot and cold together. You can imagine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were married 56 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter married at what age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And she has children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She has a daughter and a son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoshanna and David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoshanna was born in 1975, May 29. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And David was born 19-- June 15, 1975. Wait, she was born '75. 1978. 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in New York until what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you moved--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: With?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my-- with my husband. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you lived in Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you get to see your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My daughter comes often. And the summers I spend in her house. She has a house in the Hamptons, West Hamptons. So that's where I spend most of the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandchildren, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My grandchildren are wonderful. I love them, and they love me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband was retired when you came to Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he-- he had a hobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He made silver jewelry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to see that shortly. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What message-- what message do you want to leave for your children, and your great-great grandchildren, and the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be good people, and not to think that the way you want it, the way it's going to be. You have to be prepared for everything. Because I-- I had it. We had to leave and leave everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your husband had a hobby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I'm wearing here, the star, he made it. It's handmade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think I have here something. [RETRIEVING ITEM]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. This. That's a pendant. It's beautiful. Absolutely beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he make this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the terrace. On the patio. [REGARDING PENDANT] Earrings, all kinds of things. That was his hobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you share some of your pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [RETRIEVING PICTURES]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's pictures from years ago. Can I see? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you hold that up? When was that taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that, you were married how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years. No, one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: One year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This. I didn't take anything when I escape. But his sister lived in Argentina. So used to send the pictures. So that's how I got some of these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this? This is--?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken, Mrs. Winshell?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937-- no. '38. '38. And that's my husband during the war, during the escape in Central Asia. He looked like a skeleton there.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more pictures from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. It's from Tuchin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell who's in that picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin, my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hold it flat towards the-- yeah, that's it. Thank you very much?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin, my aunt, and my husband and I, and his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Point to and your husband in that picture. Can you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband [POINTING] and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Hold it flatter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must hold it to the camera, that I-- I can't see what you're pointing to. That's good. That's fine. Thank you very much. Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else have you? [RETRIEVING PICTURE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want the school in the P camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. And all of these, you have gotten-- you-- you had nothing with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That's in the P camp, that my son, they had the school. You see the way they did it? [HOLDING UP PHOTO]

speaker: interviewer
text: Point to where your son was. Do you know where he is? You have to take a look?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here, near the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And hold it up. That's right. OK. And he was about what age there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six, seven, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's from the P camp, too. Children. There were quite a few children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose children are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people from the P camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your child is--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the survivors. And my son, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I see you have some other pictures from-- of more recent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. How can I get it to you? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Won't be able to get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Why don't we stop right now, and we'll photographs the pictures on the background? This was wonderful. Thank you very, very much. That was good. We're going to do the pictures now. And you can tell us about the pictures, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Introduce her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is my daughter Betty. BETTY LONESTEIN (DAUGHTER): And I just wanted to say that this woman is-- is remarkable with all the story that-- that she has told, her courage is incredible. And she-- she's always been kind and wonderful, just as my father was. There was never any bitterness brought into our house. There was a lot of struggle and suffering, but it was never brought to us. And we were brought up with a lot of love. This is my daughter, my son-in-law at their wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's my son's picture-- my son Bob. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son's son, Jason-- my grandson. And that's my granddaughter, Jamie. That's my granddaughter, Shoshanna-- Betty's daughter. --son David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Start again.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandson David, Betty's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken? When is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a bar mitzvah. That's my husband and I at David's bar mitzvah. Since then, he passed away.
